abstract: Utilizing high-resolution mass spectrometry, we investigated the synthesis of isoquinoline within charged electrospray droplets and the complexation of cytochrome c with maltose during droplet fusion. Our objective was to evaluate the capacity of microdroplets to catalyze both covalent and non-covalent interactions at rates exceeding those found in conventional bulk solutions. In both scenarios, we observed dramatic reaction acceleration: a million-fold increase for isoquinoline synthesis and a thousand-fold increase for protein-ligand complexation. These findings suggest that conducting reactions within microdroplets (approximately 1–15 μm in diameter) serves as a robust general method for enhancing kinetic rates. While the exact underlying mechanism remains to be fully elucidated, reagent confinement and droplet evaporation appear to be primary drivers. Notably, in experiments involving fused aqueous droplets, evaporation was negligible during the flight time between fusion and analysis. This indicates that evaporation is not the sole driver of acceleration in aqueous systems, suggesting instead that the droplet–air interface plays a critical role. We propose that "microdroplet chemistry" represents a powerful alternative for driving sluggish or difficult reactions and, when paired with mass spectrometry, offers a sophisticated platform for exploring chemical and biochemical processes in highly confined environments.
